prompt: Which type of machine learning trains on labeled input-output pairs?

options: Supervised learning
Unsupervised learning
Reinforcement learning
Transfer learning

hints: Think about which type needs 'correct answers' during training.
Labeled data means each input has a known output.
explanation: Supervised learning uses labeled data — inputs paired with known correct outputs — to train a model. Unsupervised learning works with unlabeled data to find hidden structure. Reinforcement learning uses reward signals from sequential decisions.

prompt: What was the first FDA-authorized fully autonomous AI diagnostic system?

options: IDx-DR for diabetic retinopathy
CheXNet for pneumonia detection
Viz.ai for stroke triage
Paige Prostate for cancer detection

hints: It screens for a common complication of diabetes.
It was authorized in 2018 and analyzes retinal photographs.
explanation: IDx-DR (now LumineticsCore by Digital Diagnostics) was authorized in 2018 as the first FDA-authorized autonomous AI diagnostic. It screens for diabetic retinopathy from retinal photographs without requiring specialist interpretation. CheXNet was a research model, Viz.ai is triage (not autonomous diagnosis), and Paige is assistive.

prompt: Deep learning models consistently outperform classical machine learning on structured EHR data (lab values, vital signs, diagnoses).
hints: Consider what types of data deep learning excels on.
Think about gradient-boosted trees vs neural networks on tabular data.
explanation: This is false. On structured/tabular EHR data, classical ML methods like gradient-boosted trees (XGBoost, LightGBM) and logistic regression often match or outperform deep learning. Deep learning excels on unstructured data like medical images, clinical text, and waveforms where it can learn hierarchical representations from raw data.

prompt: Match each algorithm type with its most common clinical AI application.
hints: CNNs learn spatial features from pixel data.
Transformers are the architecture behind modern LLMs.
explanation: CNNs excel at image analysis due to their ability to learn spatial features. Gradient-boosted trees dominate structured EHR prediction tasks. Transformers power clinical NLP and LLMs for text processing. Logistic regression remains the standard for interpretable clinical scoring systems like LACE and Wells scores.

prompt: Categorize each clinical AI scenario as supervised learning, unsupervised learning, or reinforcement learning.

items.text: Training a model on chest X-rays labeled by radiologists as 'pneumonia' or 'normal'
Clustering ICU patients into subgroups based on physiological patterns without predefined labels
Training an agent to optimize insulin dosing through trial-and-error with simulated patient outcomes
Predicting 30-day readmission using patient records with known readmission outcomes
Identifying anomalous billing patterns without pre-labeled fraud examples
Optimizing ventilator settings by rewarding improvements in blood oxygenation

hints: Supervised learning requires labeled data with known outcomes.
Unsupervised learning finds patterns without labels.
explanation: Supervised learning requires labeled input-output pairs (chest X-rays with diagnoses, records with readmission outcomes). Unsupervised learning discovers structure in unlabeled data (patient clustering, anomaly detection). Reinforcement learning optimizes sequential decisions through reward signals (insulin dosing, ventilator management).

prompt: Arrange the steps of the clinical ML pipeline in the correct order.
hints: You must define what you're solving before collecting data.
Validation comes before deployment to ensure the model works.
explanation: The clinical ML pipeline follows a logical sequence: define the problem first, then extract relevant data, preprocess it (clean, normalize), engineer features, train and select models, validate performance (including external validation), and finally deploy with ongoing monitoring. Skipping or reordering steps — especially validation — can lead to deploying unsafe models.

prompt: A sepsis prediction model has an AUROC of 0.85. What does this mean?

options: If you randomly select one sepsis patient and one non-sepsis patient, the model will assign a higher risk score to the sepsis patient 85% of the time
The model correctly identifies 85% of all sepsis cases
85% of patients flagged by the model actually have sepsis
The model is 85% accurate overall

hints: AUROC measures discrimination — the ability to rank positives higher than negatives.
It is NOT the same as accuracy or sensitivity.
explanation: AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve) measures discrimination: the probability that a randomly selected positive case will be scored higher than a randomly selected negative case. An AUROC of 0.85 means this happens 85% of the time. It does not directly correspond to accuracy, sensitivity, or PPV, which all depend on the chosen operating threshold.

prompt: For a cancer screening AI, which metric should be prioritized?

options: High sensitivity (catching as many cancer cases as possible)
High specificity (minimizing false alarms)
High AUROC only
Equal sensitivity and specificity

hints: Consider the consequences of missing a cancer case vs a false alarm.
Screening programs are designed to cast a wide net.
explanation: In cancer screening, missing a true cancer case (false negative) has far more serious consequences than a false alarm (false positive), which leads to further testing but not direct harm. Therefore, high sensitivity (the ability to catch all true cases) is prioritized. The cost of false negatives — delayed diagnosis and treatment — outweighs the cost of false positives — additional imaging or biopsy.

prompt: A model with a high AUROC is always well-calibrated.
hints: AUROC measures discrimination (ranking). Calibration measures something different.
A model can rank patients correctly by risk but assign wrong probabilities.
explanation: False. AUROC measures discrimination — whether the model ranks positive cases higher than negative cases. Calibration measures whether predicted probabilities match actual observed frequencies. A model can have excellent AUROC (perfect ranking) but terrible calibration (predicted 80% risk when actual rate is 40%). Both must be evaluated for clinical use, especially when probabilities are communicated to clinicians or patients.

prompt: The metric that measures the proportion of patients with the disease who are correctly identified by the model is called ___.
hints: It answers: 'Of all sick patients, how many did we catch?'
It is also known as the true positive rate or recall.
explanation: Sensitivity (also called recall or true positive rate) = TP / (TP + FN). It measures the proportion of actual positive cases that the model correctly identifies. A sensitivity of 0.95 means the model catches 95% of all true cases, missing only 5%.

prompt: External validation on data from a different institution is more rigorous than internal validation using a random train/test split from the same institution.
hints: Consider how patient populations, documentation practices, and EHR systems differ between institutions.
Internal validation may overestimate real-world generalizability.
explanation: True. External validation tests whether a model generalizes beyond the institution where it was developed. Different institutions have different patient populations, documentation practices, EHR systems, and clinical workflows. A model that performs well internally may fail externally — as demonstrated by studies of Epic's sepsis model. External validation provides a more realistic estimate of how the model will perform when deployed broadly.

prompt: Match each performance metric with its definition.

pairs.right: TP / (TP + FN) — proportion of actual positives correctly identified
TN / (TN + FP) — proportion of actual negatives correctly identified
TP / (TP + FP) — proportion of positive predictions that are correct
TN / (TN + FN) — proportion of negative predictions that are correct

right: (TP + TN) / (TP + TN + FP + FN) — overall accuracy

hints: Sensitivity focuses on actual positives; specificity focuses on actual negatives.
PPV and NPV focus on the model's predictions, not the actual conditions.
explanation: Sensitivity (TP/(TP+FN)) measures how well the model catches true positives. Specificity (TN/(TN+FP)) measures how well it identifies true negatives. PPV (TP/(TP+FP)) is the positive predictive value — when the model says positive, how often is it right. NPV (TN/(TN+FN)) is the negative predictive value — when the model says negative, how often is it right. Each metric answers a different clinical question.

prompt: Arrange these AUROC values from worst to best clinical AI performance.
hints: AUROC of 0.5 is no better than a coin flip.
Higher AUROC indicates better discrimination between positive and negative cases.
explanation: AUROC ranges from 0.5 (no discrimination, equivalent to random guessing) to 1.0 (perfect discrimination). In clinical AI: 0.55 is poor (barely better than chance), 0.63 is fair, 0.72 is acceptable, 0.84 is good, and 0.91 is excellent. However, AUROC alone does not determine clinical utility — calibration, fairness, and the specific clinical context all matter.

prompt: A hospital deploys an AI sepsis prediction model that achieves high AUROC in internal validation but fires alerts on 40% of all admissions. What is the most likely clinical impact?

options: Alert fatigue — clinicians will override most alerts and may miss true positives
Improved sepsis detection because more patients are screened
Reduced mortality because early warning catches more cases
No impact because clinicians always follow AI recommendations

hints: If 40% of patients trigger alerts, are most of them actually septic?
Consider what happens when clinicians are interrupted dozens of times per shift.
explanation: With a 40% alert rate, the vast majority of alerts will be false positives (sepsis prevalence is typically 1-5% of admissions). Clinicians will quickly learn that most alerts are wrong and begin overriding them reflexively — a classic alert fatigue pattern. This was a real criticism of Epic's sepsis model, which had a high false positive rate leading to clinician disengagement.

prompt: Read the following scenario and answer the questions.

config.scenario: A health system is evaluating an AI tool for chest X-ray interpretation. The vendor reports an AUROC of 0.94 for pneumonia detection, validated on a dataset of 50,000 chest X-rays from two academic medical centers. The system is intended to prioritize the radiology worklist by flagging suspected pneumonia cases for urgent review.

questions.prompt: What is the most important validation concern before deployment?
options: The training data may not represent the health system's patient population (different demographics, imaging equipment, protocols)
The AUROC is not high enough for clinical use
Chest X-ray AI is not FDA-regulated
The vendor should have used 100,000 images instead of 50,000

questions.prompt: What type of study should the health system conduct before deployment?
options: Local validation study using their own imaging data and radiologist-confirmed labels
A survey asking radiologists if they trust the AI
A cost-benefit analysis only
No study needed since the vendor already validated the tool

hints: Academic medical centers may have different patient populations than community hospitals.
External validation on local data tests generalizability.
explanation: The most critical concern is generalizability: a model validated at academic medical centers may not perform the same at a different institution with different patient demographics, imaging equipment, and radiologist documentation practices. The health system should conduct a local validation study — testing the AI on their own data with radiologist-confirmed ground truth — before deployment. This is external validation in practice.

prompt: Most FDA-authorized clinical imaging AI products provide fully autonomous diagnoses without requiring clinician review.
hints: Think about IDx-DR — it is notable precisely because it IS autonomous.
Most imaging AI is classified as assistive.
explanation: False. The vast majority of FDA-authorized imaging AI products are assistive — they flag findings, prioritize worklists, or highlight suspicious regions for clinician review. IDx-DR (diabetic retinopathy screening) is one of very few fully autonomous systems. The distinction between assistive and autonomous has major implications for regulatory classification, clinical workflow, and liability.

prompt: A clinical NLP system extracts diagnoses from physician notes. The note says: 'No evidence of pneumonia on today's imaging.' The NLP system flags this patient as having pneumonia. What type of NLP error occurred?

options: Negation detection failure — the system did not recognize 'No evidence of' as negating the diagnosis
Named entity recognition failure — the system could not identify 'pneumonia' as a diagnosis
Temporal reasoning failure — the system misinterpreted the time reference
Assertion classification failure — the system confused a family history with a patient condition

hints: The system correctly found 'pneumonia' but missed an important qualifier.
Consider what 'No evidence of' means for the diagnosis status.
explanation: This is a negation detection failure. The NLP system correctly identified 'pneumonia' as a clinical entity (NER worked) but failed to recognize that 'No evidence of' negates the diagnosis. Negation detection is one of the most critical tasks in clinical NLP because phrases like 'denies chest pain,' 'no fever,' and 'ruled out PE' are extremely common and their misinterpretation can cause false positive flags.

prompt: Categorize each clinical AI application as primarily using classical ML or deep learning.

items.text: Predicting 30-day readmission from structured EHR variables (age, diagnoses, lab values)
Detecting pulmonary nodules on chest CT images
Generating clinical note summaries from physician dictation
Calculating a comorbidity-based mortality risk score
Classifying skin lesions from dermatoscopy photographs
Predicting ICU length of stay from admission labs and vitals

hints: Structured/tabular data (numbers, codes) works well with classical ML.
Images and text are where deep learning excels.
explanation: Classical ML (logistic regression, gradient-boosted trees) excels on structured, tabular data: EHR fields, lab values, vital signs, diagnoses. Deep learning is necessary for unstructured data: medical images (CT, dermatoscopy — using CNNs) and clinical text (note generation — using transformers). The key distinction is whether the input is structured/tabular or unstructured (images, text, waveforms).

prompt: The phenomenon where a model performs well on internal data but poorly on data from a different institution is called a failure of ___.
hints: It refers to whether the model's performance 'generalizes' to new settings.
External validation tests this property.
explanation: Generalizability (or external validity) is the ability of a model to perform well on data from institutions, populations, or time periods different from its training data. Failure of generalizability is common in clinical AI because institutions differ in patient demographics, documentation practices, EHR systems, and clinical workflows. External validation is the standard test for generalizability.

prompt: A risk prediction model assigns a 30% probability of readmission. Among all patients who received a 30% score, only 15% were actually readmitted. What does this indicate?

options: The model is poorly calibrated — it overestimates readmission risk
The model has low AUROC
The model has low sensitivity
The model has low specificity

hints: Calibration is about whether predicted probabilities match observed rates.
If the model says 30% but reality is 15%, what is the direction of the error?
explanation: This is a calibration problem. The model predicts 30% risk, but the actual observed readmission rate for patients with that score is only 15%. The model is over-predicting (overestimating risk). Poor calibration means the model's probability outputs cannot be trusted at face value, which is problematic when these probabilities are communicated to clinicians or used to allocate resources.

prompt: The process of transforming raw clinical data into meaningful model inputs (such as converting raw vital signs into '4-hour heart rate trend') is called ___.
hints: It is the step between data preprocessing and model training.
It requires clinical domain knowledge to create meaningful variables.
explanation: Feature engineering is the process of transforming raw data into meaningful model inputs (features). In clinical AI, this is where domain knowledge is most valuable — understanding that trends matter more than snapshots, that missingness is informative, and that timing has clinical significance. Feature engineering is often cited as the step where health informaticists add the most value to clinical AI teams.

prompt: Explain why a sepsis prediction model might have a high AUROC but fail to reduce sepsis mortality when deployed in a hospital. Describe at least three factors beyond model accuracy that determine clinical impact.

config.rubric: Award full marks for identifying at least three of: alert fatigue from high false positive rates, poor workflow integration, clinician distrust or lack of training, lack of actionable recommendations accompanying the alert, label leakage inflating apparent performance, population shift from training to deployment site, inappropriate operating threshold. Must demonstrate understanding that model accuracy alone is insufficient for clinical impact.
config.sampleAnswer: A high-AUROC sepsis model can fail clinically for several reasons. First, alert fatigue: if the model fires too many false positive alerts (due to a low threshold or high false positive rate), clinicians override them reflexively and miss true positives. Second, poor workflow integration: if alerts appear in an obscure dashboard rather than interrupting the clinician at the right moment in their workflow, they go unnoticed. Third, lack of actionable recommendations: an alert that says 'high sepsis risk' without suggesting specific actions (order blood cultures, check lactate, initiate fluid resuscitation) leaves clinicians unsure how to respond. Fourth, clinician distrust: without explanation of why the model flagged the patient, clinicians may dismiss the alert based on their own assessment.

hints: Think about what happens between the model producing a prediction and a patient receiving better care.
Consider the TREWS study — what made it succeed where other sepsis models failed?
explanation: Clinical AI impact depends on the entire sociotechnical system, not just model accuracy. The TREWS sepsis study at Johns Hopkins succeeded because it addressed all these factors: manageable alert volume, nurse-workflow integration, clear action protocols, and clinician training. The Epic Sepsis Model, despite reasonable AUROC, was criticized for high false positive rates and alert fatigue.

prompt: Read the scenario and answer the questions about clinical NLP deployment.

config.scenario: A health system wants to deploy an LLM-based ambient documentation tool that listens to patient-clinician conversations and generates clinical notes. The vendor claims 95% accuracy on a benchmark dataset. The system would be used across primary care, emergency, and specialty clinics.

questions.prompt: What is the most significant risk of LLM-generated clinical documentation?
options: Hallucination — the LLM may generate plausible but factually incorrect clinical details
The notes will be too short
Patients will refuse to be recorded
The tool will replace clinicians entirely

questions.prompt: What validation should the health system conduct before deployment?
options: Clinician review of generated notes compared to actual encounter content, across all planned specialties
Only checking that the notes are grammatically correct
Verifying the LLM passed medical board exams
No validation needed because the vendor provided benchmark results

hints: LLMs can generate text that sounds authoritative but contains fabricated details.
Benchmark performance may not reflect real-world clinical conversations.
explanation: The most significant risk is hallucination: LLMs can generate clinically plausible but incorrect information — fabricated symptoms, medications, or exam findings that were not discussed during the encounter. Before deployment, the health system must conduct local validation with clinician review comparing generated notes to actual encounter content across all planned clinical settings, since performance may vary by specialty, patient complexity, and conversation style.

prompt: Arrange these validation strategies from least to most rigorous for a clinical prediction model.

items: Random train/test split at one site
External validation at a different institution
correctOrder: Random train/test split at one site
External validation at a different institution

hints: Random splitting is the simplest but may overestimate performance.
Prospective validation tests the model in actual clinical conditions.
explanation: Validation rigor increases as the test data becomes more independent from training data. Random splitting is easiest but can overestimate due to data leakage. K-fold provides more stable estimates. Temporal validation mimics real deployment (predicting future from past). External validation tests generalizability across institutions. Prospective validation in actual clinical deployment is the ultimate test — measuring real-world performance including workflow integration effects.

prompt: You are a health informaticist on a clinical AI team building a readmission prediction model. The data scientist proposes using 'number of prior ED visits in the past year' as a feature. Explain why this feature might be problematic from an equity perspective and suggest an alternative approach.

config.rubric: Award full marks for: (1) identifying that ED visits correlate with access barriers, insurance status, and socioeconomic factors — not just clinical severity, (2) explaining that patients who lack primary care access may use the ED more, meaning this feature penalizes underserved populations, (3) suggesting alternatives such as using clinical severity scores, specific diagnoses, or social determinants directly rather than proxies.
config.sampleAnswer: Number of prior ED visits is problematic because it serves as a proxy for healthcare access and socioeconomic status rather than purely clinical risk. Patients who lack primary care access — often due to poverty, insurance gaps, transportation barriers, or language barriers — use the ED for conditions that insured patients manage in primary care. Using ED visits as a feature would assign higher readmission risk to underserved populations based on their access barriers, not their clinical needs. This mirrors the Optum algorithm's mistake of using cost as a proxy for need. A better approach would be to use clinical severity indicators (lab values, diagnoses, vital sign trends) and, if social determinants are relevant, measure them directly (housing instability, food insecurity, insurance status) rather than through proxy variables like ED utilization.

hints: Think about why someone might visit the ED frequently — is it always because they are sicker?
Recall the Optum algorithm case where cost was used as a proxy for need.
explanation: ED visit frequency correlates with socioeconomic status, insurance coverage, and access to primary care — not just clinical severity. Using it as a feature perpetuates the same type of bias as the Optum algorithm (using healthcare utilization as a proxy for healthcare need). The recommended approach is to use direct clinical indicators rather than utilization-based proxies that encode access disparities.

prompt: In clinical AI, a missing lab value should always be imputed with the population mean to avoid introducing bias.
hints: Is missingness random in clinical data?
Think about what it means when a clinician does NOT order a particular test.
explanation: False. In clinical data, missingness is usually informative — a missing test often means the clinician did not suspect the condition the test would detect. Imputing with the mean destroys this clinical signal and can introduce bias by treating 'test not ordered' the same as 'test result was average.' Better approaches include creating indicator variables for missingness, using last-observation-carried-forward for slow-changing values, or using algorithms that handle missing values natively (like gradient-boosted trees).

prompt: Match each clinical AI product with its primary clinical function.

pairs.right: Ambient clinical documentation from patient conversations
Real-time sepsis risk prediction in EHR

hints: Viz.ai was one of the first De Novo AI devices for acute care triage.
DAX stands for Dragon Ambient eXperience.
explanation: Viz.ai detects large vessel occlusion strokes on CT angiography and notifies the stroke team directly, reducing time-to-treatment. Nuance DAX Copilot (Dragon Ambient eXperience) generates clinical documentation from patient-clinician conversations using LLM technology. Epic's Sepsis Model is a proprietary risk prediction tool embedded in the Epic EHR. Paige Prostate was the first FDA-cleared AI for pathology, assisting pathologists in prostate biopsy analysis.

prompt: Categorize each statement as describing a problem with discrimination or a problem with calibration.

items.text: The model cannot distinguish high-risk from low-risk patients (AUROC near 0.5)
The model predicts 40% risk but only 20% of those patients actually have the outcome
The model ranks patients correctly by risk but assigns probabilities that are twice the actual event rate
Patients with and without the condition receive similar risk scores

hints: Discrimination is about ranking (can the model tell sick from healthy?).
Calibration is about accuracy of probabilities (does 30% predicted = 30% actual?).
explanation: Discrimination measures the model's ability to rank-order patients by risk (AUROC). A discrimination problem means the model cannot tell sick from healthy patients. Calibration measures whether predicted probabilities match actual observed rates. A calibration problem means the model ranks correctly but its probability outputs are unreliable. Both must be assessed for clinical use.

prompt: When a deployed clinical AI model's performance gradually decreases because clinical practice patterns or patient populations change over time, this phenomenon is called ___.
hints: The model was accurate at deployment but becomes less accurate over time.
It occurs because the real-world data 'drifts' away from what the model was trained on.
explanation: Model drift (or concept drift / data drift) occurs when the real-world data distribution changes relative to the training data. In healthcare, this can happen due to changes in clinical protocols, patient demographics, coding practices, EHR system updates, or external events like pandemics. COVID-19 caused massive drift in many clinical AI models as patient acuity, admission patterns, and care protocols changed dramatically.

prompt: Why is feature engineering considered the stage where health informaticists add the most value to clinical AI teams?

options: It requires clinical domain knowledge to transform raw data into clinically meaningful model inputs
It is the most technically complex step requiring advanced programming
Data scientists cannot perform feature engineering without medical degrees
Feature engineering is only possible with EHR data

hints: Consider what knowledge is needed to know that a creatinine trend matters more than a single value.
Think about the 'missingness is informative' concept.
explanation: Feature engineering requires understanding clinical context: knowing that trends matter more than snapshots, that timing has clinical significance (fasting vs post-meal glucose), and that missing data carries clinical signal (a missing lactate means sepsis was not suspected). This domain expertise is what health informaticists uniquely bring to clinical AI teams — bridging the gap between raw data and clinically meaningful model inputs.

prompt: A clinical AI model for sepsis prediction was evaluated on 1,000 patients. Results: True Positives (TP) = 85, False Positives (FP) = 42, True Negatives (TN) = 830, False Negatives (FN) = 43. Write Python code to compute: sensitivity (recall), specificity, positive predictive value (PPV/precision), negative predictive value (NPV), F1 score, and balanced accuracy. Then determine: is this model appropriate for clinical deployment as a primary screening tool?

config.starterCode:
# Confusion matrix values from sepsis prediction model evaluation
TP = 85   # Sepsis patients correctly identified
FP = 42   # Non-sepsis patients incorrectly flagged as sepsis
TN = 830  # Non-sepsis patients correctly identified
FN = 43   # Sepsis patients missed by the model

def compute_classification_metrics(TP, FP, TN, FN):
    # TODO: Compute each metric
    # Sensitivity = TP / (TP + FN)  -- "of all true sepsis cases, how many did we catch?"
    sensitivity = None
    
    # TODO: Specificity = TN / (TN + FP)  -- "of all true negatives, how many did we correctly identify?"
    specificity = None
    
    # TODO: PPV (Precision) = TP / (TP + FP)  -- "when we flag sepsis, how often are we right?"
    ppv = None
    
    # TODO: NPV = TN / (TN + FN)  -- "when we say no sepsis, how often are we right?"
    npv = None
    
    # TODO: F1 Score = 2 * (PPV * Sensitivity) / (PPV + Sensitivity)
    f1 = None
    
    # TODO: Balanced Accuracy = (Sensitivity + Specificity) / 2
    balanced_accuracy = None
    
    return {
        "sensitivity": sensitivity,
        "specificity": specificity, 
        "PPV": ppv,
        "NPV": npv,
        "F1": f1,
        "balanced_accuracy": balanced_accuracy
    }

metrics = compute_classification_metrics(TP, FP, TN, FN)
for name, value in metrics.items():
    print(f"{name}: {value:.3f}")

# TODO: Based on the metrics, should this model be used as primary screening?
# Consider: In sepsis, what is more costly - missing a case (FN) or false alarming (FP)?
print("\nClinical assessment: ...")
config.solutionCode:
TP = 85
FP = 42
TN = 830
FN = 43

def compute_classification_metrics(TP, FP, TN, FN):
    sensitivity = TP / (TP + FN)         # 85 / 128 = 0.664
    specificity = TN / (TN + FP)         # 830 / 872 = 0.952
    ppv = TP / (TP + FP)                 # 85 / 127 = 0.669
    npv = TN / (TN + FN)                 # 830 / 873 = 0.951
    f1 = 2 * (ppv * sensitivity) / (ppv + sensitivity)  # 0.666
    balanced_accuracy = (sensitivity + specificity) / 2  # 0.808
    
    return {
        "sensitivity": sensitivity,
        "specificity": specificity,
        "PPV": ppv,
        "NPV": npv,
        "F1": f1,
        "balanced_accuracy": balanced_accuracy
    }

metrics = compute_classification_metrics(TP, FP, TN, FN)
for name, value in metrics.items():
    print(f"{name}: {value:.3f}")

# Clinical assessment
print("""
Clinical assessment: Sensitivity of 66.4% means the model MISSES 33.6% of sepsis 
cases (43 patients). For sepsis — where missed cases can be fatal — this is 
concerning as a PRIMARY screening tool. However, specificity of 95.2% means few 
false alarms, reducing alarm fatigue. This model is better suited as a 
SUPPLEMENTARY alert (one of many signals) rather than a standalone screen. 
A clinician should still apply independent judgment. The NPV of 95.1% means 
when the model says 'no sepsis,' it is correct 95.1% of the time.
""")
hints: Sensitivity = TP / (TP + FN). Ask yourself: of all the patients who truly had sepsis, how many did the model catch?
PPV depends on the threshold of positivity — when the model says 'positive,' how often is it actually right? Think TP / (TP + FP).

explanation: In clinical AI, the choice of optimization metric is itself a clinical decision. Sensitivity (recall) captures how many true cases we detect — critical when missing a case is dangerous (sepsis, cancer screening). Specificity captures how rarely we false-alarm — critical when false positives cause harm (unnecessary procedures, alarm fatigue). PPV depends heavily on disease prevalence (Bayes' theorem) — the same model will have different PPV in a high-prevalence ICU vs. a low-prevalence primary care setting. F1 is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, useful when both matter equally.

prompt: You are evaluating an AI model for predicting 30-day hospital readmission. The model was tested on two patient groups: Black patients (n=500) and White patients (n=500). Compute demographic parity difference and equalized odds for the two groups. Determine if the model exhibits bias.

config.solutionCode:
results = {
    "Black": {"TP": 95, "FP": 78, "TN": 302, "FN": 25},
    "White": {"TP": 88, "FP": 45, "TN": 342, "FN": 25}
}

def compute_group_metrics(TP, FP, TN, FN):
    n = TP + FP + TN + FN
    return {
        "predicted_positive_rate": (TP + FP) / n,
        "sensitivity_TPR": TP / (TP + FN),
        "FPR": FP / (FP + TN)
    }

group_metrics = {}
for group, counts in results.items():
    m = compute_group_metrics(**counts)
    group_metrics[group] = m
    print(f"\n{group}:")
    for k, v in m.items():
        print(f"  {k}: {v:.3f}")

# Demographic parity difference
dpd = abs(group_metrics["Black"]["predicted_positive_rate"] - 
          group_metrics["White"]["predicted_positive_rate"])
# Equalized odds: max difference across TPR and FPR
tpr_diff = abs(group_metrics["Black"]["sensitivity_TPR"] - group_metrics["White"]["sensitivity_TPR"])
fpr_diff = abs(group_metrics["Black"]["FPR"] - group_metrics["White"]["FPR"])
eod = max(tpr_diff, fpr_diff)

print(f"\nDemographic Parity Difference: {dpd:.3f}")
print(f"Equalized Odds Difference: {eod:.3f} (FPR difference: {fpr_diff:.3f})")
print(f"\nBias flag: {'YES - FPR difference > 0.1' if fpr_diff > 0.1 else 'Within tolerance'}")
print("""
Clinical interpretation: Black patients are flagged for readmission risk at a 
higher rate (34.6% vs 26.6%), driven largely by a higher false positive rate 
(20.5% vs 11.6%). This mirrors the Obermeyer et al. (Science, 2019) finding 
where an algorithmic risk score systematically underestimated the severity 
of illness in Black patients. Here, the model over-alerts on Black patients, 
potentially leading to resource allocation decisions that don't reflect actual need,
or alternatively, generating alert fatigue for Black patients' care teams.
""")
hints: Demographic parity difference = |predicted_positive_rate_GroupA - predicted_positive_rate_GroupB|. A difference > 0.1 is a common threshold for concern.
Equalized odds requires BOTH TPR and FPR to be equal across groups. Compute each separately, then take the max difference as the equalized odds violation.

explanation: Demographic parity requires that the proportion predicted positive is equal across groups. Equalized odds requires that both TPR (sensitivity) and FPR are equal across groups. These are often in mathematical tension — satisfying one can violate the other (Chouldechova's impossibility theorem). In healthcare, the FPR disparity here is particularly concerning: Black patients are being false-alerted 75% more often than White patients, which can degrade trust in the system and cause downstream harms if clinical teams learn to discount alerts for certain groups.

prompt: Write a valid CDS Hooks 2.0 service definition (discovery endpoint response) for a clinical decision support service that checks for contraindicated medications in patients with chronic kidney disease (CKD). The service should trigger at medication prescribing time using the 'order-sign' hook.

hints: CDS Hooks defines standard hook names for clinical events. The hook that fires when a clinician signs a medication order is 'order-sign' — this is distinct from 'order-select' (when a medication is chosen but not yet signed).
FHIR prefetch templates use {{context.patientId}} as a placeholder. LOINC codes for renal function: 33914-3 = eGFR (CKD-EPI), 62238-1 = eGFR/BSA, 2160-0 = serum creatinine.

explanation: CDS Hooks is a standard for EHR-agnostic clinical decision support. The discovery endpoint (GET /cds-services) returns this JSON describing available services. 'order-sign' is the hook that fires when a clinician signs a medication order. Prefetch templates use FHIR query syntax with {{context.patientId}} as a placeholder. LOINC codes: 33914-3 = eGFR (CKD-EPI), 62238-1 = eGFR/BSA, 2160-0 = serum creatinine. The service would return CDS Cards with patient-specific recommendations when the hook fires. This standard is maintained by HL7 and supported by Epic, Oracle Health, and most major EHR vendors.

prompt:
Review this abbreviated model card for a clinical AI tool and identify ALL governance issues that would prevent approval at your hospital's clinical AI governance committee. A model card is a documentation standard for ML models. Explain each issue and what additional information or changes are required.

**Model Card: AutoDiag Sepsis Alert v2.1**
- **Task:** Binary classification — sepsis risk within 6 hours
- **Training data:** EHR data from 3 academic medical centers, 2018–2021, n=45,000 encounters
- **Validation:** Internal validation AUC = 0.84
- **Algorithm:** Gradient boosting ensemble (proprietary, black box)
- **Intended use:** Emergency department patients aged 18+
- **Update frequency:** Annually
- **Developer:** VitalSign AI Inc.
- **Contact:** support@vitalsignai.com

config.rubric: Award full marks for identifying at least 6 of the following 10 governance issues with clear explanation of why each is a problem and what would be needed to resolve it: (1) No external validation, (2) No subgroup performance data, (3) No calibration metrics, (4) No prospective clinical validation, (5) FDA regulatory status not specified, (6) Black box / no explainability plan, (7) Training data includes COVID period without acknowledgment, (8) No elderly or immunocompromised subpopulation performance, (9) No alert fatigue / expected alert rate data, (10) Vague monitoring plan with no retraining triggers.
config.sampleAnswer: This model card has at least 10 governance issues: (1) Only internal validation is reported — external validation on our institution's patient population is required before deployment. (2) No subgroup performance data by age, sex, race/ethnicity, or insurance status — a single aggregate AUC hides potentially dangerous disparities. (3) No calibration metrics — AUC measures discrimination but not whether predicted probabilities match actual sepsis rates. (4) No prospective clinical validation study — retrospective validation cannot capture alert fatigue effects, workflow integration challenges, or automation bias. (5) FDA regulatory status is not disclosed — depending on the intended use, this may require 510(k) or De Novo clearance as SaMD. (6) The algorithm is described as 'black box' with no explainability plan — this is a governance concern for most health system committees and a potential liability issue. (7) Training data from 2018-2021 includes the COVID-19 pandemic period, which dramatically altered sepsis presentation and management — this disruption is not acknowledged or characterized. (8) No performance data for elderly patients (>65), immunocompromised patients, or post-surgical patients — common high-risk ED subpopulations. (9) No information on expected alert rate per 1,000 ED visits — alert fatigue risk cannot be assessed. (10) Annual update frequency is vague — what metrics trigger an off-cycle retraining? What happens if performance degrades before the annual update?

hints: Check whether training data demographics match your patient population
What regulatory pathway does this device need from FDA?
What critical performance metrics are missing beyond AUC?
How would you validate this before deploying in your hospital?
explanation: A rigorous model card for clinical deployment should include: (1) External validation on the deployment site's own patient population — internal validation AUC is necessary but not sufficient. (2) Subgroup performance by age, sex, race/ethnicity, insurance status — a single aggregate AUC hides disparities. (3) Calibration metrics (not just discrimination) — a well-calibrated model's predicted probabilities match actual event rates. (4) Prospective clinical validation study (preferably RCT or stepped-wedge design). (5) FDA regulatory status — is this a Software as a Medical Device (SaMD)? Under the FDA's AI/ML SaMD Action Plan, this may require 510(k) clearance depending on risk class. (6) Explainability/interpretability plan — black box is a governance concern in many health systems. (7) Data cutoff during COVID (2018-2021 includes COVID disruption without acknowledging it). (8) Performance on elderly (>65), immunocompromised, or post-surgical patients — subpopulations in the ED. (9) Alert fatigue assessment — what is the expected alert rate per 1,000 ED visits? (10) Monitoring plan — annual update is vague; what triggers off-cycle retraining?
